name: How to Maximize Your Workers' Comp Settlement after a Ladder Fall
description: How to Maximize Your Workers' Comp Settlement after a Ladder Fall - Boise Idaho

step.name: Step 1
step.text:
How to Maximize Your Workers' Comp Settlement after a Ladder Fall

Falls from ladders are among the most dangerous workplace accidents, often resulting in complex fractures, spinal injuries, or traumatic brain injuries. Because the stakes are so high, insurance companies fight these claims aggressively. Here is how to protect the value of your case.

Step 1: Preserve the Evidence

If a ladder broke or was unstable, that ladder is a key piece of evidence. If possible, take photos of the ladder, its feet, the rungs, and the surrounding area where it was placed. Do not let the employer throw the ladder away, as it may be needed for an expert to inspect it for defects.

Step 2: Identify Witnesses

Did a coworker see the fall? Did someone notice the ladder was shaky earlier in the day? Get the names and phone numbers of anyone who saw the incident or the conditions leading up to it. Witness statements are vital if the employer tries to claim you were 'fooling around.'

Step 3: Follow All Medical Restrictions

If your doctor says 'no lifting over 5 pounds,' do not lift a gallon of milk. Insurance companies often hire private investigators to follow injured workers and take photos of them doing activities that contradict their medical restrictions. One photo of you carrying groceries can ruin a six-figure claim.

Step 4: Determine if a Third Party is at Fault

This is the most overlooked step. If the ladder was defective (a manufacturing flaw) or if a different contractor on the job site knocked you over, you may have a 'third-party claim.' This allows you to sue for pain and suffering—damages that are unavailable in standard workers' comp.

Step 5: Consult an Experienced Attorney

Calculating the true value of a ladder fall claim involves looking at future medical costs and potential loss of lifetime earning capacity. An attorney who specializes in work injuries knows how to use vocational experts and life-care planners to prove the full extent of your loss.
